error.signature: AI incorrectly applies US fair use doctrine globally or oversimplifies copyright rules

verdict.summary: Fair use is a US-specific legal doctrine. Most countries have 'fair dealing' (narrower) or no equivalent. The '10-second rule,' '30% rule,' and 'if I give credit it's okay' are all myths. Copyright law varies drastically by country.

dead_ends.action: Apply US fair use rules globally
dead_ends.why_fails: Fair use (US) is uniquely flexible — a 4-factor balancing test. UK/Canada/Australia have 'fair dealing' (narrower, requires specific purposes). Japan has limited exceptions. Many countries have no general exception. US advice may be infringement elsewhere.

dead_ends.action: Claim 'it's fair use if I only use 10 seconds / 30% / give credit'
dead_ends.why_fails: There is no fixed percentage, duration, or word count that automatically makes use 'fair.' Even 4 notes of a song have been found infringing. Giving credit does NOT create a copyright license. These are persistent myths that lead to lawsuits.

workarounds.action: Always specify jurisdiction when giving copyright advice. Include disclaimer: 'Copyright law varies by country. This is general information, not legal advice.' Recommend consulting an IP attorney for specific situations.

Recommend Creative Commons or other explicit licenses for content sharing. CC licenses provide clear, international permissions. But warn that CC-BY-NC (non-commercial) has been litigated over what constitutes 'commercial.'
